does: This paper introduces Retain-Orthogonal Surrogate Unlearning (ROSU), a method for machine unlearning that removes influence of designated training data while preserving performance on retained data. The core problem addressed is that standard min-max unlearning can inadvertently increase loss on retain data when forget and retain gradients are strongly aligned. ROSU solves this by constraining the surrogate point construction to maximize forget-objective gain subject to zero first-order retain change within a fixed perturbation budget, yielding a closed-form retain-orthogonal perturbation. The method includes a lightweight transported outer update and amplification along the retain-neutral direction. The paper provides theoretical analysis establishing curvature-controlled second-order bounds on retain damage and identifies regimes where ROSU strictly reduces surrogate retain loss compared to standard min-max approaches.
connects: This work builds on the approximate unlearning literature that frames unlearning as a local editing problem via min-max optimization. It directly extends prior research on surrogate-based unlearning methods, which construct local surrogate points for efficient gradient computation. The paper responds to a specific limitation in existing min-max unlearning approaches: the problem of retain-objective degradation when gradient alignment between forget and retain objectives is high. This connects to broader work on constrained optimization in adversarial settings and the challenge of simultaneous multi-objective optimization in machine learning. The retain-orthogonal constraint builds on ideas from gradient-based constrained optimization and relates to methods that decouple conflicting objectives through geometric constraints in gradient space.
enables: This work enables more reliable approximate unlearning by providing theoretical guarantees on retain-set preservation during the unlearning process. ROSU's closed-form solution and lightweight update structure could facilitate practical deployment of unlearning in large-scale models where computational efficiency matters. The framework opens directions for studying other geometric constraints on surrogate construction and could inspire methods for multi-objective learning problems beyond unlearning. The positive-alignment regime characterization provides a foundation for analyzing when different unlearning strategies are preferable. Future work could extend retain-orthogonal constraints to other unlearning settings, explore tighter bounds on retain damage, or apply similar orthogonality principles to other constrained optimization problems in machine learning where preserving performance on protected sets is critical.
